questions.question: Which statement best describes the difference between lock-freedom and wait-freedom?
options: Lock-freedom guarantees every thread completes in bounded steps; wait-freedom guarantees only system-wide progress.
Lock-freedom guarantees system-wide progress (at least one thread completes); wait-freedom guarantees every thread completes in bounded steps.
Lock-freedom uses locks; wait-freedom uses CAS.
There is no difference; the terms are synonyms.
questions.explanation: Lock-freedom ensures that at least one thread makes progress on every operation, but individual threads can starve. Wait-freedom strengthens this to guarantee that every thread completes in a bounded number of steps, regardless of contention.

questions.question: What is the consensus number of a plain atomic read/write register?
questions.explanation: Plain read/write registers have consensus number 1: they can solve consensus for at most 1 thread. To solve 2-thread wait-free consensus, you need a primitive with consensus number ≥ 2, such as test&set, fetch&add, or CAS.

questions.question: Why is the double-collect snapshot algorithm considered wait-free?
options: Because it uses CAS in a loop, and CAS is wait-free.
Because the retry loop is bounded by the number of registers, not by contention.
Because it never loops — it always succeeds on the first try.
Because it uses locks internally to prevent concurrent writes.
questions.explanation: The double-collect loop can iterate at most N+1 times (where N is the number of registers), because each iteration between two collects requires at least one writer to increment a sequence number. After all writers have been observed, the scanner must succeed. This bounded-retry property makes it wait-free.

questions.question: According to Herlihy's consensus hierarchy, which of the following is true?
options: CAS has consensus number 2, same as fetch&add.
Read/write registers can implement wait-free consensus for any number of threads.
CAS has consensus number ∞, meaning it can solve wait-free consensus for any number of threads in shared memory, but universal wait-free construction for arbitrary objects remains impossible.
fetch&add has a higher consensus number than CAS.
questions.explanation: CAS has consensus number ∞ — it can solve wait-free consensus for arbitrarily many threads in shared memory. However, this does not mean any object can be made wait-free: universal wait-free construction for arbitrary objects is impossible because it would require infinite memory to handle all possible proposals.

questions.question: What guarantee does a wait-free algorithm provide?
options: No thread ever waits for a lock
Every thread completes its operation in a bounded number of steps, regardless of other threads' behavior
No memory is allocated during the operation
questions.explanation: Wait-freedom is the strongest non-blocking guarantee: every thread finishes in a bounded number of steps, even if other threads halt. This prevents starvation but is often expensive to implement. Most practical lock-free algorithms only guarantee that some thread makes progress.
